title: The part that keeps secrets as a form of self-protection
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The secret keeper inside 

 There's a part of you that holds secrets. Maybe you've felt it. A tightening in your chest when someone asks a direct question...
htmlContent:
The secret keeper inside

There's a part of you that holds secrets. Maybe you've felt it. A tightening in your chest when someone asks a direct question. A sudden blankness when you're about to say something true. A voice that whispers "don't tell them, they'll use it against you."

I know this part well. It's been with me since childhood, long before I had words for what it was doing. It kept my father's drinking quiet. It hid the bruises. It learned to smile and say "everything's fine" while my body was screaming otherwise.

Here's the thing. This part isn't bad. It isn't broken. It isn't a liar in the way we usually mean. This part is a protector. It kept you alive when telling the truth was dangerous. When your parents couldn't handle what you needed to say. When your safety depended on keeping your mouth shut.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems therapy, calls these parts "firefighters." They rush in when something threatens to expose a vulnerable part of you. They shut things down. They redirect attention. They keep the secret at all costs. And honestly? They're often right to do so.

Think about your childhood for a moment. What happened when you told the truth? When you said "I'm scared" or "I'm sad" or "you hurt me"? If you got punished, dismissed, or shamed, your brain learned a survival lesson. Secrets equal safety. Truth equals danger. This isn't a character flaw. It's a neural pathway. It's a strategy your nervous system developed to survive an impossible situation.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying secrets are good forever. I'm saying the part that keeps them deserves your gratitude, not your judgment. That part carried something heavy so you could make it through.

How the secret keeper forms

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a memory in your head. It's a physical sensation, a posture, a way of holding your breath. The secret keeper is no different. It's a pattern that got wired into your nervous system before you had any choice in the matter.

Here's how it typically happens. You're a kid. You depend on your caregivers for everything. Food, shelter, love, safety. If your caregivers are unpredictable, neglectful, or abusive, you're in a bind. You can't leave. You can't fight back effectively. So you adapt. One adaptation is hiding parts of yourself. The parts that need comfort. The parts that ask questions. The parts that have feelings your parents can't handle.

Bessel van der Kolk's research at the Trauma Center in Boston showed that children who experience chronic invalidation learn to disconnect from their own internal experience. They stop knowing what they feel because feeling was never safe. The secret keeper becomes a guardian of that disconnection. It holds the truth so you don't have to. It carries the weight so you can function.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office years ago, unable to say what I was actually thinking. My throat would close. My mind would go blank. I'd change the subject or make a joke. My secret keeper was working overtime. And my therapist, bless her, didn't push. She just said "your system isn't ready to share that yet. That's okay."

That moment changed something. Someone finally honored the part that needed to keep secrets. Someone didn't demand I expose myself before I was ready. The secret keeper relaxed, just a little.

If you're just beginning to understand these patterns, one resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work directly addresses the inner critic and the protective parts that develop in response to childhood trauma.

The cost of keeping secrets

Here's where it gets complicated. The secret keeper saved you. But it's still running the show long after the danger is gone. And that comes with a cost.

Keeping secrets is metabolically expensive. Your body has to maintain vigilance. Your mind has to track what can and can't be said. Your nervous system stays in a low-level threat state because the truth is always waiting to come out. This is exhausting. It's why you might feel drained after social interactions. It's why intimacy feels terrifying. It's why you might have a hard time knowing what you actually want.

Janina Fisher, who specializes in trauma-informed treatment, describes this as a "split" in the psyche. One part knows the truth. Another part is terrified of it being known. These parts are in constant conflict. And you're the battlefield.

I've seen this in my own life. For years, I couldn't tell anyone about the full extent of what happened in my childhood. Not my partners. Not my closest friends. Not even myself, fully. The secret keeper had done such a good job that I'd lost access to my own history. I'd walk around with a vague sense of wrongness, a body that was always on alert, but no clear narrative about why.

Wild, right? You can be keeping a secret from yourself and not even know it.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps explain why you learned to keep so much hidden in the first place.

What the secret keeper actually needs

So what do we do with this part? We don't fire it. We don't shame it. We don't try to force it to tell everything at once.

The secret keeper needs safety. Real, embodied, felt safety. Not the kind you tell yourself you have. The kind your nervous system actually believes.

This is slow work. It's not about confessing your deepest wounds to everyone you meet. It's about finding one person, maybe a therapist or a trusted friend, who can without demanding. It's about noticing when the secret keeper activates and saying "thank you for protecting me. I'm safe now. We can take our time."

Deb Dana, who studies Polyvagal Theory, talks about "glimmers" - small moments of safety that help your nervous system shift out of protection mode. For the secret keeper, a glimmer might be someone who doesn't push when you change the subject. A friend who says "you don't have to tell me anything." A therapist who lets you sit in silence without filling it.

These small experiences accumulate. Over time, the secret keeper starts to trust that the world isn't the same as it was. That not everyone will use your truth against you. That some people can actually handle what you have to say.

But here's the hard truth. Some people can't. Some people will use your vulnerability against you. The secret keeper knows this. It's not wrong. The goal isn't to make the secret keeper disappear. The goal is to give it better information about when to protect and when it's safe to let down its guard.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz explains how all your parts, even the ones you hate, are trying to help you survive.

Learning to speak in your own time

There's a moment that happens in recovery. It's small. You might miss it if you're not paying attention. You're with someone safe, and a truth slips out. Not the whole story. Just a piece. A sentence. A feeling. And nothing terrible happens.

The world doesn't end. The person doesn't attack you. They might even say "thank you for telling me."

This is how the secret keeper learns. Not through force. Through experience. Through repeated evidence that the present is different from the past.

I've been at this for years. And I still have secrets. Parts of my story I haven't told anyone. Parts I might never tell. And that's okay. The secret keeper isn't my enemy. It's a loyal protector that stayed on duty long after its shift was supposed to end. I can appreciate it. I can thank it. And I can slowly, carefully, show it that some things have changed.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work explains why safety must come before any kind of disclosure or processing.

If you're reading this and feeling the tightness in your chest, the part that says "don't tell them what you're thinking," I see you. That part is doing its job. It's trying to keep you safe. And maybe, just maybe, you can take a breath and say "I know you're here. I know you're scared. I'm not going to make you do anything you're not ready for."

The secret keeper doesn't need to be defeated. It needs to be befriended. It needs to know that you're an adult now. That you have choices. That you can handle what comes.

And when you're ready, when the secret keeper finally relaxes its grip, you might find that the truth doesn't destroy you. It might actually set you free. Not all at once. Not perfectly. But a little more each time you let it breathe.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
